finding: An interpersonal threat activates two competing goals simultaneously (approach and self-protection); the subsequent executive control process resolves this conflict differently depending on baseline trait level, with high-trait individuals prioritizing the approach goal and low-trait individuals prioritizing the self-protection goal.

Upward counterfactual thoughts serve a dual function—increasing negative affect while also providing excuses that protect self-esteem; when they serve as excuses, they paradoxically reduce motivation for future improvement.

Individuals from a stigmatized group attend to cues signaling whether identity-based judgments are tied to their group membership in a setting; cues indicating low group representation combined with colorblindness (versus diversity-valuing) increase perceived identity-contingent threat and reduce trust.

Difficulty processing environmental stimuli shifts construal from concrete to abstract; the mechanism involves interpreting disfluently processed stimuli as psychologically more distant than fluently processed stimuli.
Linguistically specified directional relationships (via language accompanying visual targets) enhance maintenance of visual feature-location bindings during delays, whereas nonlinguistic cues do not.

Nonconsciously activated goals facilitate learning of complex environmental structure, indicating that automatic processing supports not only reproduction of learned actions but also acquisition of novel knowledge.

Gender differences in misinterpreting a social signal reflect perceptual insensitivity (inability to discriminate stimulus categories) rather than bias in decision thresholds; the same perceptual mechanism produces both types of misinterpretation.
A precise numerical anchor is adjusted away from less than a rounded anchor of the same magnitude; precise anchors are represented with finer subjective resolution, constraining iterative adjustment.

During binary decisions with competing response options, reaching trajectories toward one option are attracted toward the alternative option, indicating that decision execution reflects simultaneous influence from multiple response alternatives rather than completed decision outcomes.

People believe they ought to make optimistically biased predictions about their own future, suggesting that optimistic bias may reflect alignment with personal values rather than irrational judgment error.

Age-related decline in episodic specificity of past-event memories extends to future-event imagination; both declines correlate with diminished relational memory capacity, consistent with a unified constructive mechanism for simulating past and future.
Young children do not blindly follow a speaker's eye gaze during word learning but instead integrate gaze direction with linguistic and pragmatic context to infer referential intent.

The wisdom-of-crowds effect (averaging improves accuracy) depends on independent errors across individuals; the abstract demonstrates this principle applies to crowds of judgments from a single person.

During attentional blink (temporal attention failure), selection processes are degraded along three dissociable dimensions—efficacy (suppressed), latency (delayed), and precision (diffused)—each recovering at different rates.

Glucose availability (blood sugar) influences reliance on automatic versus effortful decision strategies; depleted glucose from prior self-control increases heuristic use, and exogenous sugar restores effortful processing.
Errors trigger defensive-motivational responses; the magnitude of the neural error signal predicts the strength of defensive reactivity to error commission.
Fair treatment is hedonically valued independently of monetary outcome; accepting unfair proposals involves emotion-regulation mechanisms that suppress negative affect.
When attention is withdrawn from visual objects, the visual system represents abstract statistical summaries (e.g., mean position) with preserved precision while local details degrade.

Implied motion depicted in static photographs activates the same direction-selective neural circuits involved in real motion perception, demonstrated by cross-adaptation between implied and real motion.

Individuals with high motivation to avoid prejudice show biased attention patterns reflecting threat arousal to members of a stigmatized group at brief stimulus durations; this bias is eliminated when the target displays positive affect.
